title: Foundations, logic and type theory
summary: Logical foundations, dependent type theory, proof terms and the formal substrate used by Lean.

targets: Map graph node semantics to Lean structures and metadata.

questions: Which informal mathematical structures need explicit universe or typeclass control?

references.label: Lean Language Reference

title: Category theory
summary: Language of objects, morphisms, functors, natural transformations, adjunctions and universal properties.

targets: Use functoriality as a formal bridge pattern.

questions: Can a proposed analogy be upgraded into a functor, adjunction or equivalence?

references.label: E. Riehl — Category Theory in Context

title: Algebra
summary: Groups, rings, modules, fields, algebras and their structure-preserving maps.

targets: Connect algebraic structure nodes to mathlib typeclass hierarchies.

questions: Which bridge requires structure, representation or invariant theory?

references.label: The Stacks Project

title: Topology
summary: Continuity, compactness, connectedness, homotopy and global qualitative structure.

targets: Track compactness and continuity assumptions in cross-domain arguments.

questions: When does a local construction fail to glue globally?

references.label: A. Hatcher — Algebraic Topology

title: Analysis
summary: Limits, differentiability, integration, measure and analytic estimates.

targets: Create toy estimates that can be reused by PDE and spectral nodes.

questions: Which estimate is the actual bottleneck of a proposed proof route?

references.label: T. Tao — Analysis I

title: Functional analysis
summary: Normed spaces, Banach and Hilbert spaces, operators and weak convergence.

targets: Formalize operator-theoretic toy bridges.

questions: Can a nonlinear problem be embedded into a linear or spectral setting?

references.label: J. B. Conway — A Course in Functional Analysis

title: Harmonic analysis
summary: Fourier analysis, singular integrals, oscillation and scale-sensitive decomposition.

targets: Identify available Fourier transform infrastructure for toy estimates.

questions: Which hidden frequency interaction blocks a global estimate?

references.label: E. M. Stein — Harmonic Analysis

title: Spectral theory
summary: Eigenvalues, spectra of operators, spectral gaps and trace information.

targets: State finite-dimensional spectral gap toy problems.

questions: Can a zero, obstruction or phase transition be reinterpreted as spectral data?

references.label: J. B. Conway — A Course in Functional Analysis
mathlib4 — Inner-product-space spectrum documentation

title: Probability and stochastic processes
summary: Random variables, stochastic dynamics, concentration and probabilistic method.

targets: Build finite probabilistic toy models for analytic or complexity barriers.

questions: Does randomness reveal the typical obstruction or only average it away?

references.label: O. Kallenberg — Foundations of Modern Probability

title: Partial differential equations
summary: Equations involving functions and derivatives across space, time and boundary data.

targets: Represent weak and strong toy PDE statements with explicit assumptions.

questions: Where does regularity transfer fail: estimates, compactness or uniqueness?

references.label: L. C. Evans — Partial Differential Equations

title: Differential geometry
summary: Manifolds, tangent structures, connections, curvature and geometric flows.

targets: Catalog which manifold prerequisites are already formalized.

questions: Can an analytic obstruction be rephrased as curvature or geometry?

references.label: J. M. Lee — Introduction to Smooth Manifolds

title: Symplectic geometry
summary: Hamiltonian geometry, phase space, symplectic forms and quantization shadows.

targets: Locate and extend formal symplectic primitives.

questions: Does a bridge preserve the phase-space structure or only the equations?

references.label: D. McDuff and D. Salamon — Introduction to Symplectic Topology

title: Algebraic geometry
summary: Varieties, schemes, sheaves, cohomology and geometric encoding of algebraic data.

targets: Connect geometric objects to cohomological bridge nodes.

questions: Which algebraic statement becomes simpler after adding geometry?

references.label: The Stacks Project

title: Arithmetic geometry
summary: Number theory through geometric objects over fields, rings and schemes.

targets: Map elliptic curve and L-function prerequisites.

questions: Can analytic rank information be represented as geometric or cohomological data?

references.label: J. H. Silverman — The Arithmetic of Elliptic Curves

title: Number theory
summary: Integers, primes, zeta functions, arithmetic functions and Diophantine structure.

targets: Formalize finite Euler product and explicit-formula toy models.

questions: Which arithmetic object has a spectral, geometric or probabilistic avatar?

references.label: J. Neukirch — Algebraic Number Theory

title: Automorphic forms
summary: Symmetries, representations and analytic functions connecting geometry and arithmetic.

targets: Find formal islands around modular forms and characters.

questions: Can a number-theoretic question be moved into representation theory?

references.label: D. Bump — Automorphic Forms and Representations

title: Representation theory
summary: Study of algebraic structures through their actions on spaces.

targets: Use actions as a bridge between algebra, geometry and physics.

questions: What symmetry is invisible in the original formulation?

references.label: W. Fulton and J. Harris — Representation Theory

title: Quantum field theory
summary: Fields, quantization, path integrals, operator algebras and renormalization heuristics.

targets: Replace informal path-integral claims with finite or algebraic toy models.

questions: Which physical heuristic has a finite, constructive or operator-algebraic version?

references.label: A. Jaffe and E. Witten — Quantum Yang-Mills Theory
R. Haag — Local Quantum Physics

title: Gauge theory
summary: Connections, curvature, principal bundles, gauge symmetry and Yang-Mills functionals.

targets: Create finite gauge-action models before continuous field theories.

questions: Can a gauge-invariant observable expose a spectral gap?

references.label: A. Jaffe and E. Witten — Quantum Yang-Mills Theory

title: General relativity
summary: Lorentzian geometry, curvature, Einstein equations and geometric analysis.

targets: Track formal prerequisites for semi-Riemannian toy geometry.

questions: Which geometric PDE tools transfer between relativity and other flows?

references.label: R. M. Wald — General Relativity

title: Complexity theory
summary: Resources needed for computation, reductions, circuits, proof systems and barriers.

targets: Formalize finite languages, reductions and toy circuit classes.

questions: Which lower-bound barrier is structural rather than technical?

references.label: S. Arora and B. Barak — Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach

title: Noncommutative geometry
summary: Geometric reasoning where coordinate algebras need not commute.

targets: Find operator algebra primitives for spectral triple toy models.

questions: Is a missing geometric space better represented by a noncommutative algebra?

references.label: A. Connes — Noncommutative Geometry

title: Optimal transport
summary: Geometry of probability measures, transport cost, Wasserstein spaces and curvature-like flow ideas.

targets: Build finite transport problems as formal toy models.

questions: Can measure geometry reveal monotonicity or compactness missing in PDE?

references.label: C. Villani — Optimal Transport: Old and New

title: Trace formula as spectral arithmetic bridge
summary: Relates spectral data, orbital/geometric data and arithmetic counting through trace identities.

targets: Formalize finite trace identities before analytic trace formulas.

questions: Which toy trace formula preserves enough structure to test RH-style analogies?

references.label: D. Bump — Automorphic Forms and Representations
E. Bombieri — Problems of the Millennium: The Riemann Hypothesis

title: Scaling and renormalization for critical PDE
summary: Uses scaling, compactness and energy flow across scales to compare PDE and field-theoretic phenomena.

targets: State a toy scale-invariant norm estimate.

questions: Can the critical obstruction be isolated as a monotone quantity or fixed point?

references.label: C. Fefferman — Existence and smoothness of the Navier-Stokes equation
K. G. Wilson and J. Kogut — The renormalization group and the epsilon expansion

title: Motives, periods and Hodge structures
summary: Connects algebraic cycles, cohomology, periods and arithmetic shadows.

targets: Represent a tiny cycle/cohomology dictionary as data.

questions: Which conjectural bridge can be tested on low-dimensional varieties?

references.label: P. Deligne — The Hodge Conjecture

title: Geometric Langlands transfer
summary: Transforms arithmetic and representation-theoretic questions into geometric categorical language.

targets: Use finite categorical analogues to model transfer.

questions: Which part of a problem becomes functorial after geometric translation?

references.label: E. Frenkel — Recent advances in the Langlands program

title: Random matrices, zeta zeros and spectra
summary: Compares statistical behavior of zeta zeros with eigenvalue statistics from random matrix ensembles.

targets: Formalize finite eigenvalue-statistic toy examples.

questions: What part of the analogy is structural, and what part is only statistical?

references.label: J. P. Keating and N. C. Snaith — Random matrix theory and zeta functions

title: Proof complexity and geometry
summary: Studies computational lower bounds through algebraic, geometric and proof-system obstructions.

targets: Formalize tiny proof systems and reductions.

questions: Can a barrier be represented as an invariant that survives reductions?

references.label: K. Mulmuley and M. Sohoni — Geometric Complexity Theory I

title: Riemann Hypothesis
summary: Nontrivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function are expected to lie on the critical line.

targets: Formalize finite Euler-product and explicit-formula toy statements.

questions: Can a spectral or cohomological avatar force critical-line symmetry?

references.label: Clay Mathematics Institute — Millennium Prize Problems

title: Yang-Mills existence and mass gap
summary: Construct quantum Yang-Mills theory on four-dimensional space-time and prove a positive mass gap.

targets: Model finite gauge systems and spectral gaps before continuum theory.

questions: Which finite or constructive model preserves the gap mechanism?

references.label: Clay Mathematics Institute — Millennium Prize Problems

title: Navier-Stokes existence and smoothness
summary: Determine global existence and smoothness, or finite-time breakdown, for 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes.

targets: Formalize a simplified energy inequality with explicit norms.

questions: Can critical-scale energy cascade be blocked by a hidden monotonicity?

references.label: Clay Mathematics Institute — Millennium Prize Problems

title: Hodge Conjecture
summary: Certain rational cohomology classes on smooth projective complex varieties should come from algebraic cycles.

targets: Represent toy algebraic cycles and cohomology classes as explicit data.

questions: Which cycle-detection problem survives in finite toy geometries?

references.label: Clay Mathematics Institute — Millennium Prize Problems

title: Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
summary: The rank of an elliptic curve over the rationals should equal the order of vanishing of its L-function at one.

targets: Map current elliptic curve formalization and finite-rank toy analogues.

questions: Can analytic rank be approximated by a formal finite or modular proxy?

references.label: Clay Mathematics Institute — Millennium Prize Problems

title: P vs NP
summary: Determine whether every efficiently verifiable problem is efficiently solvable.

targets: Formalize reductions and tiny circuit/proof systems.

questions: Which lower-bound barrier can be encoded as a robust invariant?

references.label: Clay Mathematics Institute — Millennium Prize Problems

title: Poincare Conjecture
summary: Every closed simply connected 3-manifold is homeomorphic to the 3-sphere; solved by Perelman.

targets: Use as a calibration case for topology, geometry and flow interactions.

questions: Can the graph recover why geometric flow was the decisive language?

references.label: Clay Mathematics Institute — Millennium Prize Problems

title: Constructive quantum field theory
summary: Rigorous construction and analysis of interacting quantum field theories through measures, Gaussian estimates, renormalization, cluster expansions, locality and reconstruction criteria, with every uniformity assumption made explicit.

targets: Isolate finite Gaussian, locality and cluster-expansion interfaces before making any continuum claim.

questions: Which finite estimates can be proved uniformly in volume, cutoff and renormalization scale?

references.label: J. Glimm and A. Jaffe — Quantum Physics: A Functional Integral Point of View

title: Renormalization group
summary: Multiscale transformations that replace microscopic degrees of freedom by effective actions while tracking relevant, marginal and irrelevant structures across changing resolutions.

targets: Model a finite renormalization step with explicit scale maps, supports and preserved quantities.

questions: Which estimates survive iteration with constants uniform in the number of renormalization steps?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I
K. G. Wilson and J. Kogut — The renormalization group and the epsilon expansion

title: Cluster expansion with holes and active skeletons
summary: Transforms raw local activities into renormalized polymer activities by connected Mayer covers, ultralocal integration and a second Ursell expansion. The active skeleton controls connectivity and incompatibility, while the full target controls union and locality.

targets: Preserve the exact union target and incompatibility tree until target decay has been extracted.
Prove rooted leaf-moment summation or instantiate a source-faithful Appendix-F cluster theorem.
Keep raw H, connected K, integrated K-sharp and renormalized H-sharp as distinct typed objects.

questions: Can the fixed-order second Ursell term be bounded without replacing a target fiber by a global vertex mass?
What exact source constants produce the residual loss kappa minus three kappa-zero minus three?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban III

title: Ward defect from variation of a Berezin weight
summary: Under an exact unweighted Ward identity and a Leibniz rule, the weighted defect satisfies Ber_w(QF) = -Ber((Qw)F). A coefficient norm then bounds the defect by the regulator variation instead of introducing an unexplained error parameter.

targets: Formalize the weighted Ward identity and the coefficient-l1 product estimate in a finite exterior algebra.

questions: Which concrete gauge-compatible regulator makes the weight variation small in the required polymer norm?

references.label: F. A. Berezin — Introduction to Superanalysis
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I

title: Gaussian covariance decay to collar factorization
summary: Combines support locality, exponential covariance decay and Gaussian interpolation or integration by parts to convert geometric separation into a quantitative factorization defect. The defect must become a local activity rather than being mistaken for exact independence.

targets: Prove a finite exponential-linear factorization defect, then generalize it through covariance interpolation and local derivatives.

questions: Can derivative growth and cross-covariance tails be combined without losing the coupling power needed by the raw activity bound?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II

title: Lattice units to physical distance and mass
summary: Separates graph distance from physical scale through d_phys = a n and m_phys = m_lat / a, giving the exact exponent identity m_lat n = m_phys d_phys. Continuum conclusions additionally require calibrated spacing and convergence of renormalized correlators.

targets: Prove the exact exponent identity, ceiling discretization bounds and block-scale invariance of physical mass.

questions: How should the lattice spacing be tied to the renormalization trajectory rather than chosen as an arbitrary coordinate scale?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I
K. G. Wilson and J. Kogut — The renormalization group and the epsilon expansion

title: Block-average mass term as a coercivity bridge
summary: A block Poincare estimate turns a nonnegative kinetic operator K plus a positive term a Q†Q into a coercive precision with lower bound min(1,a)/C_P. The abstract lemma isolates the model-specific task of proving the gauge block estimate.

targets: Prove the finite-dimensional coercivity lemma and a perturbation-stability corollary for self-adjoint precision operators.

questions: Which gauge slice and weighted inner products make the source block Poincare estimate true uniformly in volume?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II

title: Uniform one-step Yang-Mills RG activity bound
summary: Define the local activity produced by the actual gauge-fixed one-step renormalization integral and prove support, coupling-power and modified-metric decay estimates with constants uniform in volume, cutoff, scale, background and admissible hole geometry.

targets: Define the one-step gauge fluctuation identity and concrete raw activity before stating its decay.
Separate block coercivity, background-Hessian stability, covariance decay, collar localization and local R-operation gain into auditable inputs.

questions: Can the physical gauge producer be built without hiding the desired activity estimate inside a package field?
Which constants are uniform in volume, cutoff, scale, background and hole geometry?

references.label: A. Jaffe and E. Witten — Quantum Yang-Mills Theory
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban III

title: Polymer and cluster expansions
summary: Hard-core polymer gases and connected expansions built from Mayer factors, Ursell coefficients, Kotecky-Preiss criteria, rooted tree representations and local moment bounds, with target fibers retained whenever quantitative decay depends on the exact union.

targets: Formalize target-fiber tree sums without quotienting away distinct covers or exact unions.
Derive rooted fixed-tree assignment bounds from local parent-child moment hypotheses.

questions: Which projections preserve enough target information for volume-uniform decay estimates?
Can parent-dependent moment kernels improve constants beyond a uniform tree majorant?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I
R. Kotecký and D. Preiss — Cluster expansion for abstract polymer models

title: Target-erasure diagnostic for cluster bounds
summary: A marked-root vertex-product sum can factor into a rooted mass times an unrestricted total mass to the nth power. Once the exact target and incompatibility tree have been erased, that coarse object cannot by itself encode uniform decay in the target metric.

targets: Formalize the root-marked factorization rootedMass * totalMass^n for a finite vertex-product sum.
Construct a finite counterexample showing that equal coarse vertex budgets need not determine target-metric decay.

questions: Which variables were forgotten by a proposed majorant, and does its conclusion still depend on any of them?
Can hidden volume dependence be detected by comparing targets with the same rooted and total masses?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II
R. Kotecký and D. Preiss — Cluster expansion for abstract polymer models

title: Target-preserving Ursell summation
summary: A target-sensitive cluster estimate keeps the exact union fiber and a spanning incompatibility tree until metric stitching has extracted the desired target exponential. Only afterward may the remaining assignments and tree shapes be enlarged or summed.

targets: Define a fixed-union weighted tree term that retains both the target and parent-child incompatibility edges.
Prove target exponential extraction from metric stitching before replacing the exact fiber by a larger summation domain.
Record a reusable information-preservation lemma for cluster majorants.

questions: What is the weakest local relation that must remain visible until target decay is extracted?
Can exact-union fibers reduce the tree-shape entropy below a complete-graph majorant?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II
R. Kotecký and D. Preiss — Cluster expansion for abstract polymer models

title: Rooted leaf elimination by factorial moments
summary: Orient a tree from a marked root and sum polymer assignments from leaves upward. Rooted and parent-child incompatibility moments absorb child metric powers, yielding a fixed-tree bound governed by factorials of child counts before the remaining tree shapes are summed.

targets: Prove a generic RootedLeafKernelBound from rooted moments and parent-child incompatibility moments.
Derive the fixed-tree assignment estimate M^(2n+1) * product_v childCount(v)! under explicit nonnegative kernel hypotheses.
Separate leaf elimination from the later combinatorial sum over rooted tree shapes.
Normalize child kernels by the positive parent shifted metric and prove exact cancellation of all parent denominators through rooted child counts.

questions: Can a parent-dependent moment kernel improve the uniform moment constant?
Which rooted-tree enumeration gives the sharpest normalized bound compatible with the local kernel?
Can parent-metric normalization expose a vertexwise walk theorem without introducing a volume-dependent global sum?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban I
J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II
R. P. Stanley — Enumerative Combinatorics, Volume 2

title: Appendix-F rate-budget decomposition
summary: The first-activity decay rate can be split into residual target decay and a reserved leaf-summation budget. In the canonical bookkeeping, (kappa - kappa-zero - 2) equals (kappa - 3 kappa-zero - 3) plus (2 kappa-zero + 1).

targets: Prove the exact algebraic rate identity and monotonicity corollaries under nonnegative residual rate.
Parameterize how much decay is reserved for target extraction, leaf moments, cardinality tilts and convergence margins.
Derive a geometric order bound with ratio 4 * C2^2 * epsilon while keeping every smallness condition explicit.

questions: Can the spare unit of rate absorb an active-cardinality tilt without weakening the target exponent?
Which source constants determine whether the geometric-series window is nonempty in four dimensions?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II

title: Algebraic quantum field theory
summary: Studies relativistic quantum fields through nets of local operator algebras, isotony, microcausality, covariance, vacuum structure, modular theory and the time-slice principle rather than through a preferred particle decomposition.

targets: Model two commuting finite-dimensional local algebras and state cyclicity/separation as a bounded prototype.
Separate algebraic locality from tensor-factor assumptions in every finite formalization.

questions: Which AQFT statements can be represented faithfully before type-III von Neumann algebra infrastructure exists in Lean?

references.label: E. Witten — Notes on Some Entanglement Properties of Quantum Field Theory
R. Haag — Local Quantum Physics

title: Quantum information theory
summary: Studies quantum states, channels, entropies, correlations, recovery, error correction and operational constraints such as no-signalling, with explicit attention to uniform channels and preservation of reference-system entanglement.

targets: Formalize finite matrix channels, Kraus completeness and a no-signalling calculation.
Represent the quantifier distinction between one uniform recovery map and state-dependent recovery maps.

questions: Which finite recovery statements transfer to operator-algebraic or split AQFT settings without assuming a tensor factorization?

references.label: M. A. Nielsen and I. L. Chuang — Quantum Computation and Quantum Information
O. Fawzi and R. Renner — Quantum conditional mutual information and approximate Markov chains

title: Reeh–Schlieder vacuum nonfactorization
summary: If a vacuum vector is cyclic for one nontrivial local algebra and separating for a commuting spacelike algebra, then its state cannot factor across those algebras. Centering a distant observable and using cyclic density forces product factorization to make the distant algebra scalar.

targets: Prove the cyclic-plus-separating nonfactorization lemma for finite operator algebras under explicit nontriviality assumptions.

questions: How should cyclicity and separation be encoded without silently replacing commuting local algebras by tensor factors?

references.label: E. Witten — Notes on Some Entanglement Properties of Quantum Field Theory
R. Haag — Local Quantum Physics

title: Local complete operations preserve spacelike statistics
summary: For Kraus operators in one local algebra that commute with a distant observable, a complete nonselective operation leaves the distant expectation unchanged: completeness moves through the observable and collapses the sum to the identity. Postselection does not satisfy this conclusion without classical outcome information.

targets: Prove the finite matrix identity sum_i omega(K_i† Y K_i) = omega(Y) from commutation and sum_i K_i†K_i = 1.

questions: Which operator-algebraic hypotheses are sufficient for the same proof with infinite Kraus families or normal completely positive maps?

references.label: D. Beckman et al. — Causal and localizable quantum operations
M. A. Nielsen and I. L. Chuang — Quantum Computation and Quantum Information

title: Conditional mutual information to uniform recovery
summary: Small quantum conditional mutual information controls reconstruction of a tripartite state from a marginal by a channel acting on the conditioning system. The useful research discipline is the uniform quantifier order: one recovery map should work for every state in the chosen code or extension class.

targets: State a finite-dimensional recovery interface with an explicit reference system and uniform channel quantifiers.
Keep any split/type-III AQFT extension conditional until its operator-algebraic recovery inequality is sourced.

questions: Can a collar-conditioned recovery channel be made uniform over a finite low-energy code while preserving entanglement with a reference?

references.label: D. Sutter, O. Fawzi and R. Renner — Universal recovery map for approximate Markov chains
O. Fawzi and R. Renner — Quantum conditional mutual information and approximate Markov chains

title: Sparse Gaussian precision as an exact Markov blanket
summary: For a nondegenerate finite Gaussian split into interior, collar and exterior variables, a zero interior–exterior block in the precision matrix implies exact conditional factorization of interior and exterior given the collar. Decay of inverse diagonal blocks then bounds how collar values influence conditional means.

targets: Prove conditional Gaussian factorization for a positive-definite three-block precision matrix with a zero A-C block.
Derive a boundary-influence estimate from exponential decay of the relevant inverse block.

questions: Do gauge constraints, projectors or hard block constraints preserve the required sparse precision structure after restriction?

references.label: S. L. Lauritzen — Graphical Models

title: Exact Catalan closure of rooted child-factorial tree sums
summary: For labelled trees on n+1 vertices rooted at a fixed label, the sum of the products of child-count factorials equals n! times the nth Catalan number. This replaces the coarse Catalan bound 4^n by an exact generating function and yields a square-root closure for the second-gas majorant.

targets: Prove the exact normalized rooted child-factorial tree sum equals the Catalan number under the repository tree convention.
Derive the closed majorant B = (1 - sqrt(1 - 4 M^2 epsilon)) / (2 M) and its quadratic fixed-point equation.

questions: Does exact tree enumeration materially enlarge the admissible activity window once all source constants are instantiated?

references.label: J. Dimock — The Renormalization Group According to Balaban II
R. P. Stanley — Enumerative Combinatorics, Volume 2

title: Boundary defect from localizing a homotopy
summary: Localizing a global contraction homotopy by a support projector produces an explicit boundary defect built from commutators with that projector. In the ungraded form, the difference between the localized global identity and the localized homotopy is a sum of two commutator terms supported near the boundary.

targets: Prove the ungraded projector-localization identity in finite linear algebra.
Add the graded Koszul-sign version only after the degree conventions are fixed.

questions: Can a concrete RG-compatible local homotopy and exponentially decaying kernel make the boundary defect small in a polymer metric?

references.label: M. Doubek, B. Jurčo and J. Pulmann — Quantum L-infinity algebras and the homological perturbation lemma

title: Four-dimensional Yang–Mills as the marginal dimension
summary: Canonical power counting gives [A] = (d-2)/2 and [g] = (4-d)/2 for Yang–Mills. Thus d=4 makes the gauge coupling dimensionless and marginal, distinguishing the four-dimensional problem from superrenormalizable lower dimensions and power-counting nonrenormalizable higher dimensions.

targets: Formalize the dimensional-analysis equations as rational identities with assumptions clearly separated from the constructive field theory.

questions: Which constructive estimates exploit marginal running rather than a fictitious geometrically decaying coupling?

references.label: A. Jaffe and E. Witten — Quantum Yang-Mills Theory
K. G. Wilson and J. Kogut — The renormalization group and the epsilon expansion
